questions.question: What does longitudinal research show about relationship satisfaction trajectories over time?

options.text: All relationships inevitably decline in satisfaction after the honeymoon phase
Trajectories are highly variable — many couples maintain or increase satisfaction over a decade
Only relationships without conflict maintain high satisfaction
Initial passion intensity is the best predictor of long-term satisfaction

questions.explanation: A 10-year longitudinal study of 169 couples found four distinct trajectory patterns: high stable, moderate stable, declining, and low stable dissatisfaction. Decline is not inevitable — what matters is the behavioral patterns you build (communication, conflict management, appreciation, life stress management). Initial passion intensity actually predicts little about long-term outcomes.

questions.question: What is the bidirectional relationship-health link?

options.text: Only mental health affects relationship quality, not the reverse
Relationship quality affects health AND health affects relationship quality — both directions matter
Healthy relationships completely prevent mental illness
Physical health affects relationships but mental health does not

questions.explanation: Meta-analytic evidence shows satisfying relationships predict better immune function, lower cortisol, and reduced cardiovascular risk. The reverse is also true: depression, anxiety, chronic pain, and illness all affect relationship functioning (reducing capacity for communication, intimacy, conflict resolution). This bidirectional link means relationship problems may require individual mental/physical health treatment first or alongside relationship work.

questions.question: Which of these is an evidence-based behavioral indicator of relationship health?

options.text: Never having conflict or disagreements
Sharing all the same hobbies and interests
Repair attempts during conflict — the ability to de-escalate and re-approach after tension
Maintaining intense early-relationship passion indefinitely

questions.explanation: Repair attempts during conflict are consistently associated with relationship satisfaction in observational research. Conflict frequency is NOT a predictor (how you fight matters more than how often). Shared hobbies matter far less than values alignment. And early passion intensity predicts little about long-term outcomes — what matters is how you communicate, manage conflict, and show appreciation over time.

questions.question: What does research show about the claim 'communication skills alone keep relationships strong'?

options.text: It's fully supported — communication skills are the only thing that matters
It's an overstatement — communication improves relationship process but doesn't strongly predict long-term satisfaction on its own
It's completely false — communication skills damage relationships
Communication only matters in the first year of a relationship

questions.explanation: Longitudinal research (Lavner et al.) shows that while communication skills improve how relationships function (reduced escalation, increased clarity), they don't strongly predict long-term satisfaction on their own. Compatibility, attachment security, values alignment, and life stress management matter just as much. Communication is important but not sufficient.

questions.question: When is this relationship course NOT appropriate as your primary support?

options.text: When you want to understand your attachment patterns and build healthier communication habits
When you're in a safe relationship and want evidence-based relationship education
When there is intimate partner violence, coercive control, or you feel unsafe in your relationship
This course is appropriate for all relationship situations

questions.explanation: In relationships involving intimate partner violence (IPV) or coercive control, communication skills training is contraindicated — it can escalate danger or be weaponized by the abuser. Safety planning and specialized advocacy are needed instead (National Domestic Violence Hotline: 1-800-799-7233). The course is also not appropriate as primary support for active relationship crises (infidelity, severe escalating conflict) or severe mental illness significantly impairing functioning — these require professional couples therapy or individual treatment.

title: What Is a Healthy Relationship? The Science Behind Satisfaction - Quiz
